name: Smartmatic Controversy
description: The Smartmatic controversy centers around allegations of election fraud involving the Smartmatic company's voting machines and software, particularly during the 2020 United States presidential election. Proponents of this theory claim that Smartmatic's technology was used to manipulate vote counts, favoring one candidate over another. These claims often involve assertions of foreign interference, specifically linking Smartmatic to Venezuela and the late Hugo Chávez. The theory gained traction within certain political circles and online communities following the election, fueled by unsubstantiated reports and social media narratives. Evidence cited by proponents often includes statistical anomalies in vote counts, alleged vulnerabilities in the voting machines, and supposed connections between Smartmatic executives and foreign governments. Mainstream media outlets, fact-checkers, and election security experts have widely debunked these claims, citing a lack of credible evidence and pointing out that Smartmatic machines were only used in Los Angeles County during the 2020 election and not in any of the swing states where the most significant fraud was alleged. They argue that the claims are based on misinformation and a misunderstanding of how voting systems operate. Furthermore, Smartmatic has initiated legal action against several individuals and media organizations for defamation, seeking to clear its name and demonstrate the falsity of the allegations. The controversy highlights the challenges of combating misinformation and the impact of conspiracy theories on public trust in democratic institutions.
subtitle: Claims of election fraud and manipulation

claims.claim: Smartmatic's voting machines were used to rig the 2020 US presidential election.
claims.evidence: Statistical anomalies in vote counts and alleged vulnerabilities in the machines.
claims.criticism: Smartmatic machines were used in only one county (Los Angeles) and not in any key swing states. Statistical anomalies are often explainable by demographic shifts and voting patterns.

claims.claim: Smartmatic has ties to the Venezuelan government and the late Hugo Chávez, implying foreign interference.
claims.evidence: Past investments and business relationships with Venezuelan entities.
claims.criticism: While Smartmatic originated in Venezuela, there's no credible evidence of current government control or manipulation. Business relationships do not automatically equate to political interference.

claims.claim: The voting machines are easily hacked and manipulated, allowing for widespread fraud.
claims.evidence: Alleged security flaws and vulnerabilities discovered by cybersecurity experts.
claims.criticism: Independent audits and certifications of voting machines are conducted regularly. While vulnerabilities can exist, there's no evidence of them being exploited on a scale large enough to affect the election outcome.

keyFigures.name: Sidney Powell
keyFigures.role: Attorney who promoted the Smartmatic conspiracy theory

keyFigures.name: Dominion Voting Systems
keyFigures.role: Another company falsely accused and targeted in relation to the Smartmatic claims

keyFigures.name: Eddie Perez
keyFigures.role: Election technology expert who has debunked the claims

keyFigures.name: Smartmatic
keyFigures.role: Company at the center of the controversy, seeking to defend its reputation

mythologyConnections: The idea of a rigged election taps into anxieties about betrayal and the subversion of democratic ideals, echoing themes of deception and hidden agendas found in many mythologies.
The belief in a 'deep state' controlling events resonates with mythological narratives of secret societies and powerful cabals.

timeline.event: Widespread allegations of election fraud involving Smartmatic begin to circulate
Smartmatic files defamation lawsuits against news organizations and individuals
Legal battles continue, and the conspiracy theory persists in some circles

sources.author: Various News Outlets
sources.work: Fact-checking articles and reports on the 2020 election
sources.title: Various News Outlets
